title: Manilkara welwitschii
taxonomy: Manilkara welwitschii is a species of tree that belongs to the family Sapotaceae. This family is a group of flowering plants commonly known as the sapote or soapberry family. The Sapotaceae family is part of the larger clade of flowering plants known as the angiosperms. The angiosperms are further divided into the monocots and dicots, with the Sapotaceae family belonging to the latter group. Manilkara welwitschii is also classified under the order Ebenales and the genus Manilkara.
uses: Manilkara welwitschii, also known as the African cherry, has been used in traditional medicine for various purposes. The bark of the tree is used to treat fever, rheumatism, and other inflammatory conditions. In traditional medicine, the leaves and roots of the plant are used to treat various ailments, including digestive issues and skin problems. The plant's extracts have also been studied for their potential antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. Further research is needed to fully understand the medicinal uses of Manilkara welwitschii.

side_effects: Manilkara welwitschii is a plant species that has been used in traditional medicine, but there is limited information on its side effects. intro: The Manilkara welwitschii, also known as the M'Poso or M'Poko, is a species of tree native to tropical Africa. Traditionally used in folk medicine, the bark of Manilkara welwitschii is valued for its anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antimalarial properties, providing relief from fever, respiratory issues, and gastrointestinal problems. The bioactive constituents of Manilkara welwitschii include flavonoids, terpenoids, and alkaloids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In herbal medicine, Manilkara welwitschii is often prepared as a decoction or tincture to treat various health conditions, and its bark is also used in traditional remedies for snake bites and wounds.
